explanation: The statement says that during the Final state (assumed to mean the end or a particular final region), 'prop_0' must hold before either 'prop_1' or 'prop_2' hold. This is interpreted as, whenever we eventually reach a state where 'prop_1' or 'prop_2' holds, 'prop_0' must have occurred previously (possibly in the same state as the first occurrence of 'prop_1' or 'prop_2').

The formula says that whenever you are in a 'Final' state (if this is an atomic proposition; if not, ignore), all occurrences of prop_0 will happen only before any occurrence of prop_1 or prop_2. In LTL, this is equivalent to: if you ever see prop_1 or prop_2, then prior to that, only prop_0s are allowed in the 'Final' state. If 'Final' is not an atomic proposition, drop it and just say that all prop_0s come before any prop_1 or prop_2.

The requirement says that during periods when prop_0 holds, all instances of prop_1 must occur before any instance of prop_2 or prop_3 within those periods. This can be captured by ensuring that during prop_0, if prop_2 or prop_3 becomes true, then after that point (until prop_0 stops), prop_1 does not occur anymore. Formally, whenever prop_0 holds, whenever prop_2 or prop_3 becomes true, then from that point onward (until prop_0 ends), prop_1 does not occur.

The sentence asks that, within (i.e., during) every moment where prop_0 holds, prop_1 is true at each point before the first occurrence of either prop_2 or prop_3. This is a standard 'before' pattern limited to prop_0's scope. Globally, whenever prop_0 holds, if neither prop_2 nor prop_3 has occurred yet, then prop_1 must hold. This is expressed as: G(prop_0 -> ((!(prop_2|prop_3)) U (prop_2|prop_3) -> G(!(prop_2|prop_3) -> prop_1))). Or, more simply, for each moment where prop_0 holds, if we are before prop_2 or prop_3, then prop_1 must hold.

The requirement is that 'prop_1' always holds before both 'prop_2' and 'prop_3', but only during periods where 'prop_0' holds. That is, whenever 'prop_0' is true, up until 'prop_2' or 'prop_3' holds, 'prop_1' must be true. After 'prop_2' or 'prop_3' occurs (still under 'prop_0'), there is no requirement on 'prop_1'. This can be encoded with a global condition: at all times, when 'prop_0' holds, until ('prop_2' or 'prop_3') holds, 'prop_1' must be true.

The statement requires that whenever the system is in the 'Final state' (assumed to be represented by an atomic proposition if it is one; since not specified, we ignore an explicit 'Final state' proposition), every occurrence of prop_0 must happen before any occurrence of prop_1 or prop_2. This can be formalized as: it is always the case that if prop_1 or prop_2 occurs, then prop_0 must have previously occurred (in the past, or at least at the same moment for absence-of-past operator we're using here). In classic future-only LTL, the constraint can be modeled so that after prop_1 or prop_2 first becomes true, prop_0 is never true anymore; thus, all prop_0 must occur before the first prop_1 or prop_2.

The phrase 'During Final state, prop_1 before prop_0 and prop_2' means that in the Final state (here, assumed as a temporal context or mode), prop_1 should occur before both prop_0 and prop_2 occur. That is, neither prop_0 nor prop_2 should happen before prop_1 has happened. Usually, this is captured using the 'until' operator: both prop_0 and prop_2 cannot happen until prop_1 has occurred.

The statement requires that during the 'Final state' (assuming this means globally), prop_0 must occur before either prop_1 or prop_2 occurs. In LTL, this is represented as: (¬(prop_1 ∨ prop_2)) U prop_0, meaning that neither prop_1 nor prop_2 occurs until prop_0 has occurred.

Since 'Final' is not a valid atomic proposition, we disregard any temporal interval reference. The requirement then is: all occurrences of 'prop_0' must happen before ('prop_1' or 'prop_2') occurs. In LTL, this is expressed as: until either 'prop_1' or 'prop_2' happens, if 'prop_0' happens, it occurs before any of them; after ('prop_1' or 'prop_2') happens, 'prop_0' must not occur. This is written as: if ('prop_1' or 'prop_2') has not happened yet, then 'prop_0' implies that ('prop_1' or 'prop_2') has not happened yet; and globally after ('prop_1' or 'prop_2') happens, 'prop_0' never happens.

Since 'Final' is not a valid atomic proposition and only ['prop_0', 'prop_1', 'prop_2'] can be used, it's not possible to specify 'during Final state' in the LTL formula. However, the rest of the requirement ('all prop_0 before the prop_1 and the prop_2') can be encoded: it means that prop_1 and prop_2 cannot occur until after prop_0 has occurred at least once. This translates to: 'not (prop_1 or prop_2)' holds until 'prop_0' occurs, or formally: (!(prop_1 | prop_2) U prop_0).
